clinical_variables.primary_condition: Well‑differentiated carcinoma of the right breast (T1N1M0)

narrative:
When Maria first noticed a small, painless lump in the upper outer quadrant of her right breast, she assumed it was a benign cyst or a simple breast abscess that people over her age often develop. It had appeared a week earlier during a routine morning shower, and she could barely feel it with her fingertips. She did not experience any nipple discharge, skin dimpling, or pain, and her menstrual history—an early menarche at 12, nulliparous, and a menopause at 48—remained a silent backdrop to her everyday life. Maria’s BMI hovered at 32, and she had a long history of type‑2 diabetes that kept her daily blood sugars fluctuating between 140 and 190 mg/dL. Her hypertension was borderline controlled on lisinopril, and she had retired from smoking 10 years ago after a 20‑pack‑year habit. She also recalled her mother’s diagnosis of breast cancer at 55, and the family stories of the disease’s relentlessness loomed in her mind.

### March – “The Uneasy Night”

On the evening of March 12, Maria, 58, lay awake in her bedroom, the lump a looming reminder that it had grown in her breast in the last week. She called her primary‑care provider, Dr. Patel, and scheduled an appointment the next day. She was visibly uneasy—her palms were sweaty, her heart rate had spiked to 95 beats per minute, and she felt a low‑grade anxiety that she attributed to the fear of cancer.

### March 13 – “The First Encounter”

Dr. Patel, a board‑certified family internal‑medicine physician, conducted a focused breast exam. He noted a 1‑cm firm, mobile nodule, with no overlying skin changes, no nipple retraction, and no palpable axillary lymph nodes. He asked about systemic symptoms—weight loss, night sweats—but Maria denied any. Dr. Patel ordered an ultrasound to evaluate the lesion further and a referral to a radiologist who specialises in breast imaging. He also advised a “mammogram for definitive assessment,” stressing that although the ultrasound could provide limited details, the mammogram was necessary for staging.

Maria visited the hospital on the same day for the ultrasound. The radiologist, Dr. Wang, found a 1.5‑cm irregular, hypoechoic mass, which was classified as BI‑RADS 4c (suspicion of malignancy). Because of Maria’s BMI and breast density, the images were somewhat suboptimal, but Dr. Wang obtained clear enough pictures to flag the abnormality. “We’re going to need a core needle biopsy to be certain about what this is,” he told Maria, noting that a biopsy on a lesion of this size with the patient’s risk profile was warranted.

### March 21 – “The Diagnosis”

Maria’s biopsy results came back in two days: a well‑differentiated, invasive ductal carcinoma that measured 1.3 cm, with estrogen receptor (ER) positivity (>95 % positive by ASCO/CAP), progesterone receptor (PR) positivity (70 %+, indicating a hormone‑responsive tumour), HER2‑negative, and G2 (moderately differentiated). The sentinel node biopsy had identified one positive axillary lymph node that contained micro‑invasive disease. The staging was clear: T1N1M0, an early but node‑positive disease.

Dr. Patel and Maria discussed her options. He explained that with a positive lymph node, the standard of care called for either a completion axillary lymph node dissection or post‑lumpectomy radiotherapy with adjuvant chemotherapy, depending on her preferences and comorbidities. Maria wanted to conserve her breast, but she was apprehensive about the side effects of chemotherapy and the idea of a mastectomy. Dr. Patel and her oncologist, Dr. Lopez, decided that surgery first was the best course. They scheduled her breast‑conserving surgery (lumpectomy) with sentinel node removal for early April.

### April 9 – “The Surgery and Its Complications”

Maria entered the operating room with a mix of nervousness and relief. During the lumpectomy and sentinel node dissection, the surgeons noted that the tumour had been surrounded by a significant amount of fibrous tissue—common with the thick, fatty breast tissue in her case. The surgery proceeded without incident at first, but a few hours later Maria developed a low‑grade postoperative fever and the wound was more inflamed than usual. Post‑operative blood glucose swelled to 260 mg/dL, which likely contributed to impaired wound healing. She stayed in the hospital for three days, receiving intravenous insulin and wound care.

The pathology report confirmed the lumpectomy margins were clear, and the one lymph node had retained micro‑invasion. “We will need a radiation therapy course,” said the surgical oncologist, but a delayed start was inevitable due to Maria’s wound that had a minor infection—staphylococcus aureus on culture—and a subtle seroma accumulation that was drained in the post‑operative recovery.

### July – “Chemotherapy and Hormonal Therapy”

By early July, before the radiation therapy began, Maria’s surgeon and oncologist evaluated her readiness for systemic therapy. Given her T1N1 stage, the panel agreed that adjuvant chemotherapy was warranted. She received four cycles of dose‑adjusted AC (doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide), followed by two cycles of paclitaxel. Maria struggled with fatigue and mild neuropathy, especially after the paclitaxel cycles, but her diabetes was well‑controlled with metformin and lisinopril during this period.

Her recovery after chemotherapy was complicated by a persistent seroma and a lymphedema in her right arm. Her surgeon instituted a compression garment that, combined with her baseline hypertension, left her with occasional swollen elbows. Two months after chemotherapy, Dr. Lopez began Maria on tamoxifen 20 mg daily, anticipating an ER‑positive tumour would respond. Within three weeks, Maria noted hot flashes and increased sweating. Her weight climbed from 210 lb to 215 lb over six months—a common side effect of estrogen‑modulating drugs in patients with obesity and diabetes.

### August – “Radiation Delayed”

Radiation therapy began in late August, but due to the postoperative infection, the start was delayed by a month. When it finally started, Maria had to undergo a daily fractionated regimen for 27 fractions over five weeks. The radiation field encompassed her right breast and axilla, but the wound’s slow healing and seroma required several adjustments. The local treatment team observed that the tumour’s proximity to the skin caused some mild desquamation on the breast but no overt ulceration.

### September‑December – “Early Lurking Complications”

Throughout the radiation, Maria’s overall health remained compromised. Her blood glucose fluctuated, and her blood pressure spiked at times due to the side effects of radiation on the chest wall vessels. She experienced persistent fatigue that interfered with her ability to attend her usual walking routine. On her 2‑month post‑surgery follow‑up visit in December, Dr. Lopez performed a physical exam and noted an increase in right axillary fullness compared to before, but no new masses in the breast. Because of her DM and lymphedema, Dr. Lopez was cautious in ordering a new imaging study. After three days, MRI revealed a small, 1.2‑cm focus within the lumpectomy cavity; radiologist Dr. Nguyen commented that the area appeared suspicious—a possible regional recurrence. The MRI signals suggested a mass with high signal intensity on T2, consistent with tumour recurrence.

### December – "Recurrence"

Maria’s follow‑up in December culminated in a core needle biopsy of the recurred lesion. The same histopathologic features returned: well‑differentiated ductal carcinoma, ER+/PR+, HER2-, grade 2. The margins of her original lumpectomy were no longer clear. The pathology team recommended removal through mastectomy, with a possible reconstruction. Maria’s comorbidities—obesity, uncontrolled diabetes, previous wound infection—added to the complexity. The surgical team explained that a “radical” mastectomy might be required to clear the residual tumour and to protect the chest wall, but they had to warn her of the potential for extended healing time.

### Early 2025 – "Worsening"

By early 2025, Maria’s disease had progressed. A follow‑up bone scan, prompted by elevated alkaline phosphatase and persistent back pain, revealed lytic lesions in the thoracic vertebrae. Meanwhile, a CT of the chest showed a 3‑cm lung nodule that had been stable in the past but now displayed irregular borders and micro‑calcifications. She reported worsening fatigue, mild dyspnea, and a 12‑lb weight loss over two months—symptoms that raised red flags for metastatic spread.

Her metabolic derangements, including persistent hyperglycemia and controlled hypertension, worsened with the added stress of systemic disease. She was referred to palliative oncology and supportive care physicians who adjusted her pain management and introduced disease‑modifying agents, but the focus shifted from cure to control and comfort.

### 2025 – "Towards the End"

Maria’s disease course was an aggressive climb. At 18 months after diagnosis, her bone lesions were symptomatic, causing sharp lumbar pain that her chiropractor could no longer manage. Her liver function tests were elevated; an ultrasound of the abdomen revealed multiple hypoechoic lesions suggestive of hepatic metastases. Maria’s appetite diminished, and her energy reserves fell dramatically. The endocrine therapy was discontinued as her liver toxicity and poor appetite made further tamoxifen difficult. Her palliative team incorporated a low‑dose opioid regimen and a dietitian to address malnutrition. In the months that followed, Maria grew to prefer home care over hospital visits; her family rallied to manage her daily needs.

The disease had taken its toll. Maria’s clinical journey—beginning with a small lump, progressing through surgery, adjuvant therapy, early recurrence, and eventual disseminated disease—was a stark illustration of how comorbidities and delays in therapy can compound. Her story underscores the importance of timely wound care in diabetic patients, the hazards of radiotherapy delays, and the delicate balance of endocrine therapy side effects in a population already burdened by obesity and diabetes. The disease, once a modest 1‑cm lesion, had evolved into a widespread, difficult-to‑manage carcinoma that ultimately led to her decline.

timeline.note:
**Chief Complaint**: Evaluation of new right breast lump.

**Subjective**: Maria, 58‑year‑old female, presents with a painless, 1‑cm palpable mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast discovered 2 days ago. She denies nipple discharge, pain, erythema, or systemic symptoms. She has a significant medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.8%), hypertension (on lisinopril 10 mg daily), obesity (BMI 32), prior HRT (stopped 2 y ago), and family history of breast cancer (mother at 55).

**Objective**:
- Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 95 bpm, Temp 98.6°F, Weight 215 lb, BMI 32.
- Breast exam: Right breast, 1‑cm firm, mobile, nontender nodule in the upper outer quadrant. No overlying skin changes, no nipple discharge, no palpable axillary nodes.
- Lungs clear; cardiac exam normal.

**Assessment**: A 1‑cm, firm, mobile breast mass in a female >50 years. Differential includes benign fibroadenoma, benign cyst, atypical ductal hyperplasia, or early carcinoma. Given the patient’s age, family history, and risk factors (obesity, DM), further imaging is warranted to characterize the lesion.

**Plan**:
1. Order right breast ultrasound (same day). Schedule appointment with breast imaging specialist.
2. Reassure patient about benign possibilities but discuss that imaging findings will guide further management.
3. Continue current medications: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
4. Counsel on lifestyle: weight management, balanced diet, and exercise.
5. Follow‑up: Return to clinic within 2 weeks after ultrasound results to decide on next steps (possible core needle biopsy if suspicious). If lesion remains stable and benign on imaging, routine screening per guidelines.
6. Educate patient on self‑breast exam and signs of change to report promptly.

**Disposition**: Patient discharged with plan and imaging referral; no acute intervention needed.

---
**Key Points for Follow‑up**: Monitor imaging results, consider biopsy if suspicious, continue risk factor modification.
relevant_history: Family history: mother diagnosed with breast cancer at 55
timeline.visit_scenario: Maria arrived at the primary‑care clinic visibly concerned about a newly discovered breast lump. Dr. Patel performed a focused breast exam, noting a firm, mobile 1‑cm mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast with no overlying skin changes or palpable axillary nodes. He ruled out systemic symptoms and ordered a diagnostic ultrasound of the right breast the same day, referring her to a breast imaging specialist. Maria was instructed to schedule the ultrasound and informed it could help characterize the lesion before further steps. She left the clinic with a plan for the imaging study and reassurance that a benign cyst or abscess was still a possibility.
examination_findings: Right breast: 1‑cm firm, mobile nodule upper outer quadrant

timeline.reason_for_visit: Ultrasound evaluation of right breast lump
timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Palpable right breast lump.
History: 58‑year‑old female with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, and 20 pack‑year smoking history. Current medications include Metformin 500 mg BID and Lisinopril 10 mg daily. Patient reports a single, firm, non‑tender nodule in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast, previously 1 cm on physical examination.
Physical Exam & Imaging: Ultrasound of the right breast revealed a 1.5 cm irregular, hypoechoic lesion classified as BI‑RADS 4c, indicating high suspicion for malignancy. Image quality was suboptimal due to high body habitus and dense breast tissue.
Assessment: High‑suspicion breast lesion, most consistent with invasive ductal carcinoma or ductal carcinoma in situ. Differential includes benign entities such as phyllodes tumor or fat necrosis, but BI‑RADS 4c makes malignancy most likely.
Plan:
1. Schedule core needle biopsy of the right breast lesion for next week.
2. Pending biopsy results, arrange referral to surgical oncology for definitive surgical management.
3. Continue current pharmacologic therapy: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
4. Counsel patient on breast self‑examination, weight management, smoking cessation, and safe diabetes and hypertension control.
5. Educate patient on biopsy procedure, potential risks, and post‑procedure care.
Follow‑up: Return after biopsy for discussion of pathology results and oncology referral. If malignancy confirmed, proceed with surgical planning (lumpectomy or mastectomy and axillary assessment). If benign, continue routine surveillance.
timeline.visit_scenario: Dr. Wang reviewed the ultrasound images and identified a 1.5‑cm irregular, hypoechoic mass classified as BI‑RADS 4c, indicating high suspicion of malignancy. Due to Maria’s high body habitus and dense breast tissue, image quality was limited but diagnostic. Dr. Wang recommended a core needle biopsy of the lesion. Maria agreed and was scheduled for the biopsy the next week. Dr. Wang also noted the need for prompt referral to the surgical oncology team for definitive management if biopsy confirmed cancer.
tests_ordered: Core needle biopsy of right breast mass

timeline.reason_for_visit: Discussion of breast cancer biopsy results and treatment plan
timeline.note:
Clinic Note – Medical Oncology

Chief Complaint: Discussion of core needle biopsy results and treatment plan.

History of Present Illness:
The patient is a 58‑year‑old woman with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and obesity. She presents for review of recent core needle biopsy demonstrating a 1.3 cm, ER 95 %+, PR 70 %, HER2‑, grade 2 invasive ductal carcinoma with one sentinel lymph node showing microscopic metastatic disease (N1mi). She reports significant anxiety regarding her diagnosis and the forthcoming treatment plan.

Past Medical History:
- Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, well‑controlled on Metformin 500 mg BID.
- Hypertension, controlled on Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
- Obesity, BMI 32.

Medications: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
No known drug allergies.

Vital Signs (2024‑03‑21):
- BP: 108/66 mmHg
- HR: 86 bpm
- Temp: 98.5 °F

Physical Examination:
- Breast: Right upper outer quadrant 1.3 cm mobile, firm mass, unchanged since last visit.
- Axilla: No clinically palpable lymphadenopathy.

Assessment:
- Invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast, T1c N1mi M0, Stage IIA.
- Favorable hormone receptor status (ER > 90 %, PR > 70 %) and HER2‑negativity.
- Surgical candidate for breast‑conserving surgery with sentinel node removal.

Plan:
1. Surgical: Schedule right breast lumpectomy with sentinel node dissection in early April.
2. Adjuvant Therapy: Post‑operative whole breast irradiation and adjuvant systemic chemotherapy (e.g., dose‑dense AC/Taxol) to be initiated 2–4 weeks after surgery.
3. Endocrine Therapy: Initiate adjuvant endocrine therapy with aromatase inhibitor (exemestane 25 mg daily) following chemotherapy.
4. Diabetic Management: Continue Metformin; monitor fasting glucose and HbA1c at each visit and prior to surgery.
5. Blood Pressure: Continue Lisinopril; check BP peri‑operatively.
6. Lifestyle: Reinforce weight management, dietary counseling, and aerobic exercise.
7. Psychosocial: Provide written educational material and encourage participation in support groups; offer to arrange a short‑term referral to a counseling service if anxiety persists.

Follow‑up:
- Return 2 weeks after surgery for discussion of pathology and initiation of adjuvant therapy, or sooner if patient develops new breast pain, nipple discharge, or systemic symptoms.
- Contact clinic for any urgent concerns.

Signature: Dr. Lopez
relevant_history: Biopsy indicated well‑differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma, 1.3 cm, ER+, PR+, HER2‑, G2
Sentinel node biopsy showed one positive node with micro‑invasion
timeline.visit_scenario: Dr. Lopez reviewed the core needle biopsy pathology confirming a well‑differentiated, ER+/PR+ invasive ductal carcinoma (T1N1M0). He explained the disease staging and discussed surgical options: a breast‑conserving lumpectomy with sentinel node removal versus mastectomy. Given Maria’s desire to preserve her breast and her favorable tumor biology, they agreed on a lumpectomy. Dr. Lopez also discussed adjuvant therapy, noting that because of the positive node status, a combination of postoperative radiation and systemic chemotherapy would be recommended. Maria was advised to prepare for surgery in early April and to stay closely monitored for her diabetes while awaiting the procedure.
test_results: Biopsy: invasive ductal carcinoma, 1.3 cm, ER 95%+, PR 70%+, HER2−, G2, one lymph node positive with micro‑invasion
treatments_administered: Patient education regarding surgery and adjuvant therapy
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Diagnosis: Well‑differentiated, ER+/PR+ invasive ductal carcinoma, T1N1M0.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Post‑operative evaluation after lumpectomy and sentinel node dissection
timeline.note:
Chief Complaint: Post‑operative evaluation 3 days after right lumpectomy and sentinel node dissection.

History of Present Illness: 58‑year‑old female presents with low‑grade fever (38.2 °C), mild erythema and tenderness over the right breast surgical incision, and hyperglycemia (capillary glucose 260 mg/dL). Three days post‑op, a seroma pocket is noted. Wound culture grew Staphylococcus aureus. The patient has a known history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity (BMI 32), and a 20 pack‑year smoking history. Current therapy includes Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily, intravenous insulin per glucose, and exemestane 25 mg daily. No known drug allergies.

Physical Examination: Vital signs – BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 94 bpm, Temp 38.2 °C. Right breast: surgical incision with subtle erythema, mild tenderness, and palpable seroma pocket. No purulent drainage. Axilla: no new lymphadenopathy. Extra‑oral exam normal.

Assessment: 1) Post‑operative surgical site infection (coag‑negative Staphylococcus aureus). 2) Seroma formation at lumpectomy site. 3) Impaired wound healing secondary to uncontrolled hyperglycemia.

Plan:
- Continue intravenous insulin titration to maintain capillary glucose <150 mg/dL.
- Start oral dicloxacillin 500 mg q6h for 7 days; complete full antibiotic course.
- Aspirate seroma with image‑guided technique; place closed suction drain if residual >20 mL.
- Educate patient on wound care, signs of worsening infection, and the importance of tight glycemic control.
- Defer adjuvant whole‑breast irradiation until the incision is fully healed (no erythema, drainage, or fever). 
- Arrange for diabetes team review to intensify metformin or add basal insulin once post‑op inflammation subsides.
- Schedule follow‑up in 7 days for wound assessment; earlier return if fever >38.5 °C, increased erythema, or drainage.
- Continue antihypertensive therapy and endocrine therapy as per oncologic guidelines.
- Provide resources for smoking cessation counseling and weight reduction.

The patient was instructed to monitor daily temperature, wound appearance, and blood glucose. She is advised to contact the clinic immediately if fever rises or wound changes. No contraindication to continuing current hormonal therapy.
relevant_history: Operative findings: fibrous breast tissue, clear margins confirmed by pathology
Post‑op infection: Staphylococcus aureus culture positive
timeline.visit_scenario: Three days after the lumpectomy, Maria presented with a low‑grade fever, mild wound erythema, and hyperglycemia. The surgical oncologist reviewed the pathology report confirming clear lumpectomy margins; the sentinel node was positive but micro‑invasive. A wound culture grew Staphylococcus aureus, and a small seroma was present. The team started intravenous insulin to control blood glucose, prescribed oral antibiotics for the wound infection, and scheduled wound care with aspiration of the seroma. He noted the need to postpone adjuvant radiation until wound healing was adequate and emphasized the importance of glycemic control to facilitate optimal wound recovery.
examination_findings: Right breast: surgical incision with subtle erythema and mild tenderness
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Post‑operative complications: wound infection, seroma; margins clear; sentinel node positive; radiation delayed.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Initiation of adjuvant chemotherapy and endocrine therapy
timeline.note:
Patient is a 58‑year‑old female with Stage IIA ER+ invasive ductal carcinoma currently receiving adjuvant therapy. Post‑lumpectomy and radiation, wound status is healed and the patient is stable to initiate chemotherapy. She has been started on a dose‑adjusted AC regimen (doxorubicin 30 mg/m² + cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m²) for four cycles followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m² for two cycles.

Assessment:
1) Chemotherapy‑related fatigue after first AC cycle – likely due to myelosuppression and adenosine accumulation.
2) Mild peripheral neuropathy after paclitaxel – expected dose‑related toxic effect.
3) Hot flashes and 5‑lb weight gain – consistent with tamoxifen therapy.
4) Mild leukopenia (WBC 3.1 × 10⁹/L) after first cycle – within acceptable limits but warrants monitoring.
5) Cardiac function remains normal with LVEF 60% – no anthracycline toxicity noted.

Plan:
1) Continue current chemotherapy schedule.
2) Monitor CBC/hemoglobin at baseline and pre‑cycle, LFTs pre‑cycle, and ECHO every 6 cycles or sooner if clinical suspicion.
3) Manage neuropathy with duloxetine 30 mg PO qd or gabapentin 300 mg PO bid if symptoms worsen; educate on protective footwear.
4) Continue tamoxifen 20 mg daily.
5) Address hot flashes with low‑dose antihistamine (hydroxyzine 25 mg PO bid) or a SERM‑related agent if tolerated.
6) Reinforce weight management through diet and moderate exercise; consider referral to dietitian.
7) Continue metformin 500 mg BID and adjust insulin sliding scale per glucose logs to maintain BG <150 mg/dL during chemo.
8) Maintain compression garment for right‑arm lymphedema, monitor arm circumference for escalation.
9) Educate patient on signs of neutropenic fever; advise immediate contact if fever >38°C or chills.
10) Schedule next follow‑up in 2 weeks for CBC, discussion of neuropathy, and adjustment of insulin dosing.

Patient’s overall functional status is good, and she is motivated to continue treatment. All medication changes and expectations have been discussed and she is aware of next steps.
relevant_history: Completion of lumpectomy and radiation therapy (started August 29)
Current blood sugars controlled with metformin
timeline.visit_scenario: Dr. Lopez reviewed Maria’s recovery after lumpectomy and radiation. He confirmed that her wound was healed and she was stable enough to begin adjuvant chemotherapy. He initiated a dose‑adjusted AC regimen (doxorubicin 30 mg/m² + cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m²) for four cycles, followed by paclitaxel 80 mg/m² weekly for two cycles, while closely monitoring for cardiotoxicity and neuropathy. Maria reported fatigue after the first AC cycle and mild numbness in her feet after the paclitaxel cycles. She experienced warm‑thunder hot flashes and an uptick in weight attributable to tamoxifen. The oncology team emphasized the importance of continuing her metformin for glycemic control and adjusted her insulin management during chemotherapy cycles.
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Adjuvant chemo completed with manageable toxicity; endocrine therapy started.

timeline.reason_for_visit: Evaluation of suspicious post‑radiation breast cavity lesion
timeline.note:
Date: 2024-12-20

Chief Complaint: Evaluation of suspicious post‑radiation breast cavity lesion.

History of Present Illness: The patient is a 58‑year‑old female with a history of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma treated with lumpectomy, sentinel node dissection, adjuvant chemotherapy (AC + paclitaxel), tamoxifen, and recently exemestane. She presents for a routine two‑month post‑surgery follow‑up. The patient reports mild right axillary fullness but denies new breast lumps, nipple discharge, or skin changes. She continues to experience persistent fatigue. No changes in weight or new symptoms of lymphedema.

Past Medical History: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Hypertension, Obesity, Lymphedema. Recent radiation therapy completed in late August. No current infections.

Medications: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Tamoxifen 20 mg daily, Exemestane 25 mg daily, Dicloxacillin 500 mg PO q6h, Intravenous insulin sliding scale, Doxorubicin 30 mg/m2 IV, Cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m2 IV, Paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV.

Allergies: NKDA.

Physical Examination:
- Vital signs: BP 118/69, HR 84, Temp 98.3°F.
- Right breast: Scar tissue from lumpectomy, no palpable masses.
- Axilla: Mild fullness, no discrete nodes, no erythema or tenderness.

Assessment:
1. 1.2‑cm T2‑hyperintense lesion within the lumpectomy cavity (MRI), ER+/PR+, HER2−, grade 2 on core needle biopsy – suspicious for local recurrence.
2. Persistent fatigue likely multifactorial (post‑chemo, endocrine therapy, diabetes, anemia).
3. Mild axillary fullness – likely residual post‑surgical edema or reactive changes.

Differential Diagnoses for cavity lesion:
- Locoregional recurrence of breast cancer.
- Post‑radiation fibrosis/hematoma.
- Benign postoperative scar tissue.

Plan:
A. Oncologic Management:
- Discussed surgical re‑excision with curative intent if feasible; patient scheduled for board‑approved surgical consult. If margins are clean and no residual disease, consider repeat lumpectomy or mastectomy based on surgeon recommendation.
- Awaiting pathology from surgical specimen if re‑excision performed.
- Continue adjuvant endocrine therapy: tamoxifen continued for 5 years total, exemestane continued given HER2‑ status and menopausal status.
- No changes to chemotherapy at this time; next cycle of paclitaxel scheduled in 2 weeks.
- Continue monitoring CBC, LFTs, and cardiac function every cycle.

B. Symptom Management:
- Persistent fatigue: order CBC, CMP, ferritin, 25‑OH vitamin D; consider adjusting insulin or adding basal insulin if hyperglycemic.
- Mild peripheral neuropathy: continue duloxetine 60 mg PO nightly; review dose if pain increases.
- Hot flashes: prescribe hydroxyzine 50 mg PO nightly PRN.

C. Lymphedema Management:
- Reinforce compression garment use.
- Refer to physical therapy for manual lymphatic drainage and exercise.
- Schedule lymphedema clinic in 4 weeks.

D. Imaging and Surveillance:
- Repeat mammogram of right breast in 6 weeks to correlate with MRI findings.
- Consider ultrasound of axilla if patient develops new swelling.

E. Follow‑up:
- Oncology clinic in 2 weeks for chemotherapy planning.
- Surgical oncology consult by next oncology visit.
- Endocrine clinic in 1 month.
- Lymphedema clinic in 4 weeks.

Patient educated on signs of infection, neutropenic fever, importance of blood pressure control, and adherence to endocrine therapy.

Signed:
Dr. Lopez, MD
Oncology
relevant_history: No new imaging abnormality in routine follow‑up
timeline.visit_scenario: During a routine two‑month post‑surgery follow‑up, Dr. Lopez felt an increase in right axillary fullness. Concerned about potential local recurrence, he ordered a breast MRI. Three days later, radiologist Dr. Nguyen reported a 1.2‑cm hyper‑intense T2 lesion within the lumpectomy cavity, suggestive of possible recurrence. Dr. Lopez performed a core needle biopsy which returned the same histology—well‑differentiated ductal carcinoma, ER+/PR+, HER2‑, grade 2—with infiltrative margins. He explained to Maria that mastectomy was recommended to remove residual tumour and achieve clear margins. He also addressed her lymphedema management and the possibility of reconstructive options. Maria was given information about reconstructive timelines and potential complications.
examination_findings: Right breast: scar tissue, no palpable masses
Axilla: mild fullness, no discrete nodes
test_results: MRI: 1.2‑cm lesion in lumpectomy cavity, T2 hyperintense, suspicious for recurrence
treatments_administered: Core needle biopsy (returned ER+/PR+, HER2‑, G2)

timeline.reason_for_visit: Assessment of bone and lung metastatic disease
timeline.note:
**Chief Complaint**: Assessment of bone and lung metastatic disease.

**Subjective**: Maria reports chronic lower back pain radiating to the lumbar spine, increased shortness of breath on exertion, unintentional weight loss of 12 lb over the past two months, and increased fatigue. She denies fevers, night sweats, or new onset of neurological deficits.

**Past Medical History**: Type 2 DM, hypertension, obesity, post‑mastectomy with reconstruction, ongoing lymphedema.

**Medications**: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily, hydromorphone 2 mg PRN for pain, calcium + vitamin D supplement, compression garment for lymphedema.

**Allergies**: NKDA.

**Review of Systems**: Negative for upper GI bleeding, chest pain, or recent infection. Positive for back pain, dyspnea on exertion, weight loss, and fatigue.

**Objective**
- **Vitals**: BP 130/78 mmHg, HR 90 bpm, Temp 98.0 °F, RR 18 breaths/min, O₂ sat 96% RA.
- **General**: Appears anxious, in moderate distress from pain.
- **Back**: Tenderness over T4–T6 with limited range of motion; no focal neuro exam abnormality.
- **Chest**: Mild inspiratory crackles bilaterally; no wheeze.
- **Breast**: Well‑healed mastectomy scar, no signs of recurrence.
- **Labs**: Alkaline phosphatase 320 IU/L (elevated); CBC pending.

**Assessment**: 1) Metastatic breast cancer with lytic bone metastases at T4–T6 explaining chronic back pain. 2) Newly identified 3 cm irregular pulmonary nodule with micro‑calcifications, likely metastatic disease or primary lung carcinoma. 3) Elevated alkaline phosphatase consistent with increased bone turnover and potential impending fracture risk.

**Plan**
1. **Pain Management**: Continue hydromorphone PRN; instruct patient to taper to 2 mg q4 h PRN and consider basal‑bolus oxycodone 5 mg/10 mg BID if inadequate control. Add gabapentin 300 mg BID for neuropathic component of pain.
2. **Bone‑Health**: Initiate denosumab 120 mg SC q4 weeks (or zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q12 weeks) to reduce skeletal‑related events; continue calcium 1,200 mg and vitamin D 800 IU daily.
3. **Systemic Therapy**: Start palbociclib 125 mg orally once daily for 3 days per month (low‑dose regimen) combined with endocrine therapy (tamoxifen continues, consider addition of letrozole if disease progresses).
4. **Supportive Care**: Referral to dietician for weight‑gain strategies; schedule home‑based palliative care visit to assess symptom control and psychosocial needs.
5. **Diagnostics**: Repeat CBC, CMP, and LDH in 3 weeks to monitor for myelosuppression and organ function. Consider PET‑CT in 3 months to reassess disease burden.
6. **Hospice Consultation**: Offer hospice services; patient consented to continue home‑based care with family.
7. **Follow‑Up**: Oncology follow‑up in 4 weeks; palliative home‑care visit within 48 h.

**Disposition**: Maria agreed to the above plan, will maintain home‑based pain management schedule, and will contact the palliative care team if pain escalates or new symptoms develop.

**Signature**: 
Dr. Singh, M.D.

---

***Note***: This plan reflects the limited efficacy of systemic therapies at this advanced stage and prioritizes symptom control and quality of life.
symptoms: Lower back pain radiating to lumbar spine
Unintentional weight loss of 12 lb over two months
relevant_history: Bone scan revealing lytic thoracic vertebral lesions
CT chest showing new 3‑cm irregular lung nodule
timeline.visit_scenario: Dr. Singh evaluated Maria for advanced metastatic breast disease. The bone scan had identified multiple lytic lesions in T4–T6 vertebrae, explaining her chronic back pain. An incidental 3‑cm pulmonary nodule on chest CT had irregular borders and micro‑calcifications, raising suspicion for metastatic spread. Dr. Singh discussed the limited efficacy of systemic therapies at this stage and transitioned care to a palliative oncology approach, focusing on symptom control. He adjusted her pain regimen, reviewed anti‑resorptive therapy, and initiated low‑dose palbociclib in conjunction with endocrine therapy where tolerated. Nutritional referral was made, and a hospice consultation was offered. Maria agreed to continue home‑based care with her family.
examination_findings: Spine: tenderness over thoracic vertebrae, limited range of motion
test_results: Bone scan: lytic lesions at T4–T6
Chest CT: 3‑cm irregular lung nodule, micro‑calcifications
timeline.disease_progression_notes: Metastatic progression to bone and lung; palliative care initiated.

notes.content:
**Chief Complaint**: Evaluation of new right breast lump.

**Subjective**: Maria, 58‑year‑old female, presents with a painless, 1‑cm palpable mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast discovered 2 days ago. She denies nipple discharge, pain, erythema, or systemic symptoms. She has a significant medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.8%), hypertension (on lisinopril 10 mg daily), obesity (BMI 32), prior HRT (stopped 2 y ago), and family history of breast cancer (mother at 55).

**Objective**:
- Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 95 bpm, Temp 98.6°F, Weight 215 lb, BMI 32.
- Breast exam: Right breast, 1‑cm firm, mobile, nontender nodule in the upper outer quadrant. No overlying skin changes, no nipple discharge, no palpable axillary nodes.
- Lungs clear; cardiac exam normal.

**Assessment**: A 1‑cm, firm, mobile breast mass in a female >50 years. Differential includes benign fibroadenoma, benign cyst, atypical ductal hyperplasia, or early carcinoma. Given the patient’s age, family history, and risk factors (obesity, DM), further imaging is warranted to characterize the lesion.

**Plan**:
1. Order right breast ultrasound (same day). Schedule appointment with breast imaging specialist.
2. Reassure patient about benign possibilities but discuss that imaging findings will guide further management.
3. Continue current medications: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
4. Counsel on lifestyle: weight management, balanced diet, and exercise.
5. Follow‑up: Return to clinic within 2 weeks after ultrasound results to decide on next steps (possible core needle biopsy if suspicious). If lesion remains stable and benign on imaging, routine screening per guidelines.
6. Educate patient on self‑breast exam and signs of change to report promptly.

**Disposition**: Patient discharged with plan and imaging referral; no acute intervention needed.

---
**Key Points for Follow‑up**: Monitor imaging results, consider biopsy if suspicious, continue risk factor modification.
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule right breast ultrasound within 2 weeks
Return to office to review imaging results and decide on core needle biopsy if indicated
Continue current medications
Adopt lifestyle modifications to address obesity and diabetes
Perform routine breast cancer screening per guidelines

notes.content:
Chief Complaint: Palpable right breast lump.
History: 58‑year‑old female with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity, and 20 pack‑year smoking history. Current medications include Metformin 500 mg BID and Lisinopril 10 mg daily. Patient reports a single, firm, non‑tender nodule in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast, previously 1 cm on physical examination.
Physical Exam & Imaging: Ultrasound of the right breast revealed a 1.5 cm irregular, hypoechoic lesion classified as BI‑RADS 4c, indicating high suspicion for malignancy. Image quality was suboptimal due to high body habitus and dense breast tissue.
Assessment: High‑suspicion breast lesion, most consistent with invasive ductal carcinoma or ductal carcinoma in situ. Differential includes benign entities such as phyllodes tumor or fat necrosis, but BI‑RADS 4c makes malignancy most likely.
Plan:
1. Schedule core needle biopsy of the right breast lesion for next week.
2. Pending biopsy results, arrange referral to surgical oncology for definitive surgical management.
3. Continue current pharmacologic therapy: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
4. Counsel patient on breast self‑examination, weight management, smoking cessation, and safe diabetes and hypertension control.
5. Educate patient on biopsy procedure, potential risks, and post‑procedure care.
Follow‑up: Return after biopsy for discussion of pathology results and oncology referral. If malignancy confirmed, proceed with surgical planning (lumpectomy or mastectomy and axillary assessment). If benign, continue routine surveillance.
tests_ordered: Core needle biopsy of right breast mass
follow_up_recommendations: Biopsy next week
Referral to surgical oncology pending biopsy results
Return for pathology discussion and oncology consultation
Continue Metformin and Lisinopril
Weight management counseling
Smoking cessation resources

notes.content:
Clinic Note – Medical Oncology

Chief Complaint: Discussion of core needle biopsy results and treatment plan.

History of Present Illness:
The patient is a 58‑year‑old woman with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and obesity. She presents for review of recent core needle biopsy demonstrating a 1.3 cm, ER 95 %+, PR 70 %, HER2‑, grade 2 invasive ductal carcinoma with one sentinel lymph node showing microscopic metastatic disease (N1mi). She reports significant anxiety regarding her diagnosis and the forthcoming treatment plan.

Past Medical History:
- Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, well‑controlled on Metformin 500 mg BID.
- Hypertension, controlled on Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
- Obesity, BMI 32.

Medications: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily.
No known drug allergies.

Vital Signs (2024‑03‑21):
- BP: 108/66 mmHg
- HR: 86 bpm
- Temp: 98.5 °F

Physical Examination:
- Breast: Right upper outer quadrant 1.3 cm mobile, firm mass, unchanged since last visit.
- Axilla: No clinically palpable lymphadenopathy.

Assessment:
- Invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast, T1c N1mi M0, Stage IIA.
- Favorable hormone receptor status (ER > 90 %, PR > 70 %) and HER2‑negativity.
- Surgical candidate for breast‑conserving surgery with sentinel node removal.

Plan:
1. Surgical: Schedule right breast lumpectomy with sentinel node dissection in early April.
2. Adjuvant Therapy: Post‑operative whole breast irradiation and adjuvant systemic chemotherapy (e.g., dose‑dense AC/Taxol) to be initiated 2–4 weeks after surgery.
3. Endocrine Therapy: Initiate adjuvant endocrine therapy with aromatase inhibitor (exemestane 25 mg daily) following chemotherapy.
4. Diabetic Management: Continue Metformin; monitor fasting glucose and HbA1c at each visit and prior to surgery.
5. Blood Pressure: Continue Lisinopril; check BP peri‑operatively.
6. Lifestyle: Reinforce weight management, dietary counseling, and aerobic exercise.
7. Psychosocial: Provide written educational material and encourage participation in support groups; offer to arrange a short‑term referral to a counseling service if anxiety persists.

Follow‑up:
- Return 2 weeks after surgery for discussion of pathology and initiation of adjuvant therapy, or sooner if patient develops new breast pain, nipple discharge, or systemic symptoms.
- Contact clinic for any urgent concerns.

Signature: Dr. Lopez
diagnoses_considered: Invasive ductal carcinoma, ER/PR positive, HER2 negative, T1c N1mi (Stage IIA)

notes.content:
Chief Complaint: Post‑operative evaluation 3 days after right lumpectomy and sentinel node dissection.

History of Present Illness: 58‑year‑old female presents with low‑grade fever (38.2 °C), mild erythema and tenderness over the right breast surgical incision, and hyperglycemia (capillary glucose 260 mg/dL). Three days post‑op, a seroma pocket is noted. Wound culture grew Staphylococcus aureus. The patient has a known history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, obesity (BMI 32), and a 20 pack‑year smoking history. Current therapy includes Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily, intravenous insulin per glucose, and exemestane 25 mg daily. No known drug allergies.

Physical Examination: Vital signs – BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 94 bpm, Temp 38.2 °C. Right breast: surgical incision with subtle erythema, mild tenderness, and palpable seroma pocket. No purulent drainage. Axilla: no new lymphadenopathy. Extra‑oral exam normal.

Assessment: 1) Post‑operative surgical site infection (coag‑negative Staphylococcus aureus). 2) Seroma formation at lumpectomy site. 3) Impaired wound healing secondary to uncontrolled hyperglycemia.

Plan:
- Continue intravenous insulin titration to maintain capillary glucose <150 mg/dL.
- Start oral dicloxacillin 500 mg q6h for 7 days; complete full antibiotic course.
- Aspirate seroma with image‑guided technique; place closed suction drain if residual >20 mL.
- Educate patient on wound care, signs of worsening infection, and the importance of tight glycemic control.
- Defer adjuvant whole‑breast irradiation until the incision is fully healed (no erythema, drainage, or fever). 
- Arrange for diabetes team review to intensify metformin or add basal insulin once post‑op inflammation subsides.
- Schedule follow‑up in 7 days for wound assessment; earlier return if fever >38.5 °C, increased erythema, or drainage.
- Continue antihypertensive therapy and endocrine therapy as per oncologic guidelines.
- Provide resources for smoking cessation counseling and weight reduction.

The patient was instructed to monitor daily temperature, wound appearance, and blood glucose. She is advised to contact the clinic immediately if fever rises or wound changes. No contraindication to continuing current hormonal therapy.
diagnoses_considered: Post‑operative surgical site infection due to Staphylococcus aureus
Impaired wound healing secondary to hyperglycemia
medications: Intravenous insulin (sliding scale) per glucose readings
follow_up_recommendations: Follow‑up wound assessment daily; monitor temperature and erythema
Continue insulin regimen to maintain glucose <150 mg/dL; contact diabetes team as needed
Consider second seroma aspiration if volume >20 mL or persistent swelling
Defer adjuvant whole breast irradiation until wound is fully healed
Return to clinic in 7 days; sooner if fevers rise or wound worsens
Refer to nutrition for weight‑loss counseling
Continue hypertension and endocrine therapy unchanged

notes.content:
Patient is a 58‑year‑old female with Stage IIA ER+ invasive ductal carcinoma currently receiving adjuvant therapy. Post‑lumpectomy and radiation, wound status is healed and the patient is stable to initiate chemotherapy. She has been started on a dose‑adjusted AC regimen (doxorubicin 30 mg/m² + cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m²) for four cycles followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m² for two cycles.

Assessment:
1) Chemotherapy‑related fatigue after first AC cycle – likely due to myelosuppression and adenosine accumulation.
2) Mild peripheral neuropathy after paclitaxel – expected dose‑related toxic effect.
3) Hot flashes and 5‑lb weight gain – consistent with tamoxifen therapy.
4) Mild leukopenia (WBC 3.1 × 10⁹/L) after first cycle – within acceptable limits but warrants monitoring.
5) Cardiac function remains normal with LVEF 60% – no anthracycline toxicity noted.

Plan:
1) Continue current chemotherapy schedule.
2) Monitor CBC/hemoglobin at baseline and pre‑cycle, LFTs pre‑cycle, and ECHO every 6 cycles or sooner if clinical suspicion.
3) Manage neuropathy with duloxetine 30 mg PO qd or gabapentin 300 mg PO bid if symptoms worsen; educate on protective footwear.
4) Continue tamoxifen 20 mg daily.
5) Address hot flashes with low‑dose antihistamine (hydroxyzine 25 mg PO bid) or a SERM‑related agent if tolerated.
6) Reinforce weight management through diet and moderate exercise; consider referral to dietitian.
7) Continue metformin 500 mg BID and adjust insulin sliding scale per glucose logs to maintain BG <150 mg/dL during chemo.
8) Maintain compression garment for right‑arm lymphedema, monitor arm circumference for escalation.
9) Educate patient on signs of neutropenic fever; advise immediate contact if fever >38°C or chills.
10) Schedule next follow‑up in 2 weeks for CBC, discussion of neuropathy, and adjustment of insulin dosing.

Patient’s overall functional status is good, and she is motivated to continue treatment. All medication changes and expectations have been discussed and she is aware of next steps.
diagnoses_considered: Tamoxifen‑associated hot flashes and weight gain
follow_up_recommendations: Schedule CBC and LFT before each subsequent chemotherapy cycle
Repeat ECHO every 6 cycles or if clinically indicated
Monitor for febrile neutropenia; contact clinic if fever >38°C
Assess and document neuropathy severity; consider duloxetine or gabapentin
Reevaluate weight and diet; consider dietitian referral
Continue lymphedema compression and monitor forearm circumference
Adjust insulin dosing as needed during chemo
Return in 2 weeks for follow‑up and next cycle planning

notes.content:
Date: 2024-12-20

Chief Complaint: Evaluation of suspicious post‑radiation breast cavity lesion.

History of Present Illness: The patient is a 58‑year‑old female with a history of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma treated with lumpectomy, sentinel node dissection, adjuvant chemotherapy (AC + paclitaxel), tamoxifen, and recently exemestane. She presents for a routine two‑month post‑surgery follow‑up. The patient reports mild right axillary fullness but denies new breast lumps, nipple discharge, or skin changes. She continues to experience persistent fatigue. No changes in weight or new symptoms of lymphedema.

Past Medical History: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Hypertension, Obesity, Lymphedema. Recent radiation therapy completed in late August. No current infections.

Medications: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily, Tamoxifen 20 mg daily, Exemestane 25 mg daily, Dicloxacillin 500 mg PO q6h, Intravenous insulin sliding scale, Doxorubicin 30 mg/m2 IV, Cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m2 IV, Paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV.

Allergies: NKDA.

Physical Examination:
- Vital signs: BP 118/69, HR 84, Temp 98.3°F.
- Right breast: Scar tissue from lumpectomy, no palpable masses.
- Axilla: Mild fullness, no discrete nodes, no erythema or tenderness.

Assessment:
1. 1.2‑cm T2‑hyperintense lesion within the lumpectomy cavity (MRI), ER+/PR+, HER2−, grade 2 on core needle biopsy – suspicious for local recurrence.
2. Persistent fatigue likely multifactorial (post‑chemo, endocrine therapy, diabetes, anemia).
3. Mild axillary fullness – likely residual post‑surgical edema or reactive changes.

Differential Diagnoses for cavity lesion:
- Locoregional recurrence of breast cancer.
- Post‑radiation fibrosis/hematoma.
- Benign postoperative scar tissue.

Plan:
A. Oncologic Management:
- Discussed surgical re‑excision with curative intent if feasible; patient scheduled for board‑approved surgical consult. If margins are clean and no residual disease, consider repeat lumpectomy or mastectomy based on surgeon recommendation.
- Awaiting pathology from surgical specimen if re‑excision performed.
- Continue adjuvant endocrine therapy: tamoxifen continued for 5 years total, exemestane continued given HER2‑ status and menopausal status.
- No changes to chemotherapy at this time; next cycle of paclitaxel scheduled in 2 weeks.
- Continue monitoring CBC, LFTs, and cardiac function every cycle.

B. Symptom Management:
- Persistent fatigue: order CBC, CMP, ferritin, 25‑OH vitamin D; consider adjusting insulin or adding basal insulin if hyperglycemic.
- Mild peripheral neuropathy: continue duloxetine 60 mg PO nightly; review dose if pain increases.
- Hot flashes: prescribe hydroxyzine 50 mg PO nightly PRN.

C. Lymphedema Management:
- Reinforce compression garment use.
- Refer to physical therapy for manual lymphatic drainage and exercise.
- Schedule lymphedema clinic in 4 weeks.

D. Imaging and Surveillance:
- Repeat mammogram of right breast in 6 weeks to correlate with MRI findings.
- Consider ultrasound of axilla if patient develops new swelling.

E. Follow‑up:
- Oncology clinic in 2 weeks for chemotherapy planning.
- Surgical oncology consult by next oncology visit.
- Endocrine clinic in 1 month.
- Lymphedema clinic in 4 weeks.

Patient educated on signs of infection, neutropenic fever, importance of blood pressure control, and adherence to endocrine therapy.

Signed:
Dr. Lopez, MD
Oncology

**Chief Complaint**: Assessment of bone and lung metastatic disease.

**Subjective**: Maria reports chronic lower back pain radiating to the lumbar spine, increased shortness of breath on exertion, unintentional weight loss of 12 lb over the past two months, and increased fatigue. She denies fevers, night sweats, or new onset of neurological deficits.

**Past Medical History**: Type 2 DM, hypertension, obesity, post‑mastectomy with reconstruction, ongoing lymphedema.

**Medications**: Metformin 500 mg BID, Lisinopril 10 mg daily, hydromorphone 2 mg PRN for pain, calcium + vitamin D supplement, compression garment for lymphedema.

**Allergies**: NKDA.

**Review of Systems**: Negative for upper GI bleeding, chest pain, or recent infection. Positive for back pain, dyspnea on exertion, weight loss, and fatigue.

**Objective**
- **Vitals**: BP 130/78 mmHg, HR 90 bpm, Temp 98.0 °F, RR 18 breaths/min, O₂ sat 96% RA.
- **General**: Appears anxious, in moderate distress from pain.
- **Back**: Tenderness over T4–T6 with limited range of motion; no focal neuro exam abnormality.
- **Chest**: Mild inspiratory crackles bilaterally; no wheeze.
- **Breast**: Well‑healed mastectomy scar, no signs of recurrence.
- **Labs**: Alkaline phosphatase 320 IU/L (elevated); CBC pending.

**Assessment**: 1) Metastatic breast cancer with lytic bone metastases at T4–T6 explaining chronic back pain. 2) Newly identified 3 cm irregular pulmonary nodule with micro‑calcifications, likely metastatic disease or primary lung carcinoma. 3) Elevated alkaline phosphatase consistent with increased bone turnover and potential impending fracture risk.

**Plan**
1. **Pain Management**: Continue hydromorphone PRN; instruct patient to taper to 2 mg q4 h PRN and consider basal‑bolus oxycodone 5 mg/10 mg BID if inadequate control. Add gabapentin 300 mg BID for neuropathic component of pain.
2. **Bone‑Health**: Initiate denosumab 120 mg SC q4 weeks (or zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q12 weeks) to reduce skeletal‑related events; continue calcium 1,200 mg and vitamin D 800 IU daily.
3. **Systemic Therapy**: Start palbociclib 125 mg orally once daily for 3 days per month (low‑dose regimen) combined with endocrine therapy (tamoxifen continues, consider addition of letrozole if disease progresses).
4. **Supportive Care**: Referral to dietician for weight‑gain strategies; schedule home‑based palliative care visit to assess symptom control and psychosocial needs.
5. **Diagnostics**: Repeat CBC, CMP, and LDH in 3 weeks to monitor for myelosuppression and organ function. Consider PET‑CT in 3 months to reassess disease burden.
6. **Hospice Consultation**: Offer hospice services; patient consented to continue home‑based care with family.
7. **Follow‑Up**: Oncology follow‑up in 4 weeks; palliative home‑care visit within 48 h.

**Disposition**: Maria agreed to the above plan, will maintain home‑based pain management schedule, and will contact the palliative care team if pain escalates or new symptoms develop.

**Signature**: 
Dr. Singh, M.D.

---

***Note***: This plan reflects the limited efficacy of systemic therapies at this advanced stage and prioritizes symptom control and quality of life.
symptoms_reported: Lower back pain radiating to lumbar spine
Unintentional weight loss of 12 lb over two months
follow_up_recommendations: Repeat CBC, CMP, LDH in 3 weeks
Schedule oncology follow‑up in 4 weeks
Arrange home‑based palliative care visit within 48 hours
Consider additional bone‑protective therapy if fracture risk increases
Reassess nutritional status with dietician
Hospice consultation pending patient preference

prior_visit_summaries: 2024-03-13: 58‑year‑old female presented with a painless 1‑cm right breast lump. Vitals: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 95 bpm, Weight 215 lb, BMI 32. Breast exam: firm, mobile, nontender upper outer quadrant nodule, no skin changes or nipple discharge. Ordered right breast ultrasound; prescribed Metformin 500 mg BID and continued Lisinopril 10 mg daily. Counselled on weight management, diet, exercise, self‑breast exam; scheduled follow‑up in 2 weeks upon imaging results.

2024-03-13: Ultrasound of the right breast identified a 1.5 cm irregular hypoechoic lesion classified as BI‑RADS 04c. Plan to schedule core needle biopsy the following week. Continue Metformin 500 mg BID and Lisinopril 10 mg daily. Counselling on breast self‑examination, weight management, and smoking cessation was reinforced.

2024-03-21: Review of core needle biopsy results revealing 1.3 cm ER 95%+, PR 70% invasive ductal carcinoma, T1c N1mi M0 stage IIA. Planned right breast lumpectomy with sentinel node dissection, postoperative whole breast irradiation, adjuvant chemotherapy (dose‑dense AC/Taxol), and endocrine therapy with exemestane 25 mg daily. Continued current diabetes and hypertension therapy, addressed anxiety, and discussed psychosocial support.

2024-04-09: Post‑operative evaluation 3 days after right breast lumpectomy and sentinel node dissection. Vitals: BP 112/68 mmHg, HR 94 bpm, Temp 38.2°C. Physical exam revealed mild erythema and tenderness over incision with palpable seroma pocket; no purulent drainage. Wound culture positive for Staphylococcus aureus. Capillary glucose 260 mg/dL, continued intravenous insulin titration to <150 mg/dL. Started oral dicloxacillin 500 mg q6h for 7 days. Seroma aspirated and closed suction drain placed if residual >20 mL. Advised deferment of whole‑breast irradiation until incision heals. Diabetes team review scheduled to adjust metformin or add basal insulin. Follow‑up in 7 days for wound assessment. Continued antihypertensive Lisinopril and endocrine therapy exemestane.

2024-07-05: 58‑year‑old female with Stage IIA ER+ invasive ductal carcinoma, post‑lumpectomy and radiation, wound healed. Initiated adjuvant chemotherapy: dose‑adjusted AC (doxorubicin 30 mg/m² + cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m²) 4 cycles followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m² 2 cycles. Started tamoxifen 20 mg daily. Vitals: BP 120/70 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, Temp 98.4 °F. Symptoms: fatigue, mild peripheral neuropathy, hot flashes, 5 lb weight gain. Labs ordered: CBC with differential, LFTs, baseline cardiac echo. Plan: continue chemotherapy schedule, monitor CBC, LFTs, and ECHO every 6 cycles; manage neuropathy with duloxetine or gabapentin; address hot flashes with antihistamine; monitor insulin dosing; maintain compression garment; educate on neutropenic fever. Follow‑up in 2 weeks.

2024-12-20: 58‑year‑old female evaluated for a 1.2‑cm MRI‑identified lesion within the right breast lumpectomy cavity. Core needle biopsy showed ER+/PR+, HER2−, grade 2 invasive ductal carcinoma suspicious for recurrence. Continued endocrine therapy with tamoxifen 20 mg daily and exemestane 25 mg daily. Planned surgical re‑excision consultation; next paclitaxel dose scheduled in 2 weeks. Added duloxetine 60 mg nightly for neuropathy and hydroxyzine 50 mg nightly PRN for hot flashes. Ordered CBC, CMP, ferritin, vitamin D labs; repeat mammogram in 6 weeks. Reinforced compression garment use and scheduled lymphedema referral. No changes to diabetes, hypertension, or antibiotic therapy.

2025-03-15: 58‑year‑old female evaluated for new metastatic bone and pulmonary disease. Chief complaint of chronic lower back pain, shortness of breath on exertion, unintentional weight loss, and fatigue. Vitals: BP 130/78 mmHg, HR 90 bpm, Temp 98.0 °F. Physical exam: tenderness over T4–T6, mild inspiratory crackles bilaterally, well‑healed mastectomy scar. Labs: serum alkaline phosphatase 320 IU/L (elevated); CBC pending. Started hydromorphone 2 mg PRN for pain, gabapentin 300 mg BID for neuropathic pain, denosumab 120 mg SC q4 weeks (or zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q12 weeks) for bone protection, palbociclib 125 mg daily, calcium 1200 mg daily, and vitamin D 800 IU daily. Scheduled bone scan, chest CT, serum alkaline phosphatase, and CBC. Referred to dietician for weight‑gain strategies and arranged home‑based palliative care visit within 48 hours. Plan for oncology follow‑up in 4 weeks.
